retrieved_docs.title: Specific Phobia -- Evaluation -- Screen for Child Anxiety–Related Emotional Disorders
retrieved_docs.content: Each response is scored between 0 and 2, with a total score of 25 or higher having high sensitivity and specificity for discriminating between anxiety and nonanxiety disorders. [39] A 55% or higher reduction in the total score with treatment best predicts treatment response and a 60% or higher reduction in parent SCARED scores predicts remission. [39] There is a modified version of SCARED, the Screen for Adult Anxiety Related Disorders (SCAARED), available to screen adults for similar anxiety disorders as children.

retrieved_docs.title: Specific Phobia -- Evaluation -- Screen for Child Anxiety–Related Emotional Disorders
retrieved_docs.content: The SCARED assessment tool can be used free of charge with an acceptable time burden on clinicians and families, making it an excellent tool for diagnosing and managing anxiety disorders in children. SCARED cutoffs can also be used to guide treatment. For example, an insufficient reduction in the SCARED score after an adequate trial of behavioral therapy may indicate the need for pharmacotherapy. [39] Studies have shown some discordance in the information provided by the child and parent on this questionnaire without apparent contributory factors. [38] More research is warranted to understand the cause of "low informant agreement" and what factors contribute to this discrepancy. Still, the SCARED assessment tool is considered a stable, reliable, valid, and sensitive measure of anxiety, despite the informant discrepancy, which interestingly also stays stable over time. The SCARED screening tool has shown strict measurement invariance and solid test-retest reliability.

retrieved_docs.title: Specific Phobia -- Differential Diagnosis -- Separation Anxiety Disorder
retrieved_docs.content: Separation anxiety is primarily a disorder of childhood, however, it can occur in adults as well. Separation anxiety disorder describes the fear of being separated from an attachment figure, while specific phobia results in anxiety when exposed to a phobic stimulus.

retrieved_docs.title: Specific Phobia -- Evaluation -- Screen for Child Anxiety–Related Emotional Disorders
retrieved_docs.content: Screen for Child Anxiety–Related Emotional Disorders (SCARED) is one of the most commonly used assessment tools for diagnosing anxiety disorders in children. SCARED is a child and parent self-report measure evaluated in numerous settings worldwide. [39] Various versions and revisions of the questionnaire have been developed. The most commonly used version consists of 41 questions. The total score is based on 5 subscale scores for the most common pediatric anxiety disorders: Generalized anxiety disorder Social phobia Social anxiety disorder (SAD) Somatic symptoms or panic disorder School phobia.

retrieved_docs.title: Specific Phobia -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Parenting behaviors implicate cross-generational influences on the development of childhood anxiety. These parenting behavioral systems include vicarious learning, social referencing, and modeling of parental anxiety. Furthermore, overly protective and overly critical parenting styles, parental response to child anxiety, and family accommodation of a child's anxiety all contribute to the conditioning and development of childhood anxiety. [14] Children of parents with depression and anxiety disorders are at a higher risk of developing a depressive or anxiety disorder. [15] Another mechanism for developing a specific phobia is through modeling, in which a person observes a reaction in another person and internalizes that other person's fears or warnings about the dangers of a specific object or situation. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Specific Phobia -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Anxiety disorders are among the most common mental health disorders, with an estimated prevalence of 5% to 34% worldwide, affecting both children and adults. [17] [18] [19] Lifetime 12-month prevalence estimates for specific phobia range from 7.7% to 12.5%; these estimates are maintained through different surveyed populations around the world. [20] [21] [22] [23] [24] [25] The incidence rates for specific phobia are estimated to be 26.9% for individuals between 20 and 50 years. [25] [26] Prevalence rates are higher in females compared to males, [27] and they decrease with age. [28] [29] Sequentially, the most frequent types of specific phobias are those with stimuli related to animals, the natural environment, and then blood-injection-injury–related stimuli. [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Separation Anxiety Disorder -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Anxiety disorders are among the most common pediatric mental health disorders, with an estimated prevalence of 5 to 25% worldwide. [18] SAD is the most frequently diagnosed childhood anxiety disorder, said to account for 50% of the referrals for anxiety-related mental health treatment. [18] The prevalence of SAD is estimated at 4% in population-based studies, with an increase to 7.6% in pediatric clinical samples. [9] Clinical data suggests that 4.1% of children exhibit a clinical level of separation anxiety, with approximately one-third persisting into adulthood if left untreated. [18] The average onset age is approximately 6 years, making it 1 of the earliest anxiety disorders to present in children. [9] In a United States-based study, SAD was found to have the highest lifetime prevalence at 6.7% of all anxiety disorders when specific and social phobias are excluded. [19] The lifetime prevalence of adult SAD is 6.6%, with 77.5% of the patients reporting onset in adulthood. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Specific Phobia -- Evaluation -- Screening Tools
retrieved_docs.content: Multiple screening tools for anxiety disorders exist and have wide availability and validation. Generalized anxiety disorder screeners are common and widely available for adult screening, however most broader anxiety disorder screeners were designed to screen for anxiety disorders in children. When there is difficulty in obtaining the full diagnostic criteria from the interview alone, implementing a validated screening tool can be helpful in the diagnosis of specific phobia and in identifying possible comorbid conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: Claustrophobia -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Claustrophobia evaluation starts with a patient who presents with the symptoms of fear. It needs to be determined if the fear represents a phobia, normal fear, a general medical condition, or an anxiety disorder. Questions in the initial history taking part in the assessment that assesses how the fear impacts the person's development, daily life, and family dynamics are vital to distinguishing anxiety and phobias. For children, sample questions are: How do the child's parents respond to their fear? Can the child or parent recall the exact trigger of the anxiety? Is there any interruption in the child's daily schedule more than 3 times a day? [14] Questionnaires are options to evaluate claustrophobia. The Spielberger psychology questionnaire consists of 20 items; each scored from 20 to 80, which measures apparent anxiety. [15] There are three commonly used scales to assess symptoms of anxiety and fear in the younger population: The revised Children's Manifest Scale, the Fear Survey Schedule for Children-Revised, and the State-Trait Anxiety Inventory for Children. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Specific Phobia -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The presentation of specific phobia is variable. The chief complaint may be related to anxiety or impaired functioning in their social responsibilities due to symptoms of their illness. The chief complaint will likely be related to significant fear or anxiety in the presence of a specific situation or object, known as the phobic stimulus. It is essential to investigate if there is a single phobic stimulus or multiple. The anxiety reaction to the phobic stimulus must be greater than the expected reaction for the general population. In presence of the phobic stimuli, the anxiety must be severe, and it can produce panic attacks in certain cases. Anticipation of exposure to phobic stimuli may also induce anxiety. The anxiety reaction is anticipated to evoke nearly every time there is exposure to the phobic stimulus. Children may express fear or anxiety by crying, tantrums, freezing, or clinging.

retrieved_docs.content: Differential diagnoses for specific phobia include:

retrieved_docs.title: Specific Phobia -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The diagnosis and management of specific phobia require the efforts of a coordinated interprofessional healthcare team. Both pediatric and adult providers are likely to encounter patients suffering from specific phobia. Multiple studies show that patients with anxiety disorders tend to have more frequent medical visits and increased healthcare utilization rates, especially for comorbid medical conditions or somatic complaints. Individuals with suspected specific phobia should be promptly referred for a behavioral health evaluation by a psychiatrist.

retrieved_docs.title: Specific Phobia -- Evaluation -- Anxiety Disorder Interview Schedule
retrieved_docs.content: A CSR of 4 or higher is required to provide a particular diagnosis. If the child and parent interviews yield different diagnoses and CSRs, the interviewer makes a composite diagnosis using recommended guidelines in the clinician manual. The ADIS's parent and child versions possess high inter-rater and test-retest reliability. One study reported almost perfect agreement on both the child and parent interview for diagnosing an anxiety disorder using ADIS. They also report almost perfect agreement regarding the severity of the primary diagnosis. [40] The ADIS is considered the gold-standard diagnostic evaluation for anxiety disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: Generalized Anxiety Disorder -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Childhood anxiety occurs in about 1 in 4 children at some time between the ages of 13 and 18 years. The median age at onset is 11 years. However, the lifetime prevalence of a severe anxiety disorder in children ages 13 to 18 is approximately 6%. The general prevalence in children under 18 years is between 5.7% and 12.8%. The prevalence is approximately twice as high among women as among men. [4] [5] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Separation Anxiety Disorder -- Evaluation -- Screening Tools
retrieved_docs.content: Screen for Child Anxiety-Related Emotional Disorders (SCARED): SCARED is 1 of the most commonly used assessment tools for diagnosing anxiety disorders in children. SCARED is a child and parent self-report measure evaluated in numerous settings worldwide. [32] Various versions/revisions of the questionnaire have been developed. The most commonly used version consists of 41 questions. The total score is based on 5 subscale scores for the most common pediatric anxiety disorders: generalized anxiety disorder, social phobia, SAD, somatic symptoms/panic disorder, and school phobia. Each response is scored between 0 and 2, with a total score of 25 or higher having high sensitivity and specificity for discriminating between anxiety and non-anxiety disorders. [32] A 55% or higher reduction in the total score with treatment best predicts treatment response and a 60% or higher reduction in SCARED-parent scores predicts remission. [32]

retrieved_docs.title: Specific Phobia -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The etiology of most psychiatric conditions involves various degrees and types of biological, psychological, and social contributors. Although psychological factors particularly impact the development of anxiety disorders, there are multiple biological components to pathological anxiety. The study of inheritance patterns has revealed a general familial aggregation among major anxiety disorders. [10] Twin studies have demonstrated stronger inheritance patterns for monozygotic twins compared to dizygotic twins, suggesting a genetic component to the development of pathological anxiety. [11] Although the study of anxiety and gene-environment interactions is limited, it is known that epigenetic mechanisms, particularly DNA methylation, contribute to mediating transcription factors for stress-related genes, which may underlie the development of pathological anxiety. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1607
retrieved_docs.content: Developmental disorders constitute a broad group of conditions that affect neurodevelopmental processes. Within this overarching category, other psychiatric disorders are regarded as a subset, indicating that these psychiatric conditions share developmental origins or characteristics. Moreover, other psychiatric disorders are also classified as a form of رُهاب (phobia), reflecting that some of these conditions present primarily as intense, irrational fears. This dual classification underscores the overlap between developmental psychopathology and specific anxiety‑related disorders, highlighting the need for clinicians to consider developmental factors when diagnosing and managing phobic psychiatric presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1576
retrieved_docs.content: Anxiety disorders comprise a broad diagnostic category that includes several specific subtypes. One such subtype, Anxiety disorder due to another medical condition, is classified under the related category Anxiety disorder due to known physiological condition and is defined by explicit diagnostic criteria that are incorporated into the overall diagnostic framework for anxiety disorders. Another distinct entity, Other specified transient mental disorders due to conditions classified elsewhere, is recognized as a child category within the anxiety disorders hierarchy. Recognizing these hierarchical relationships and the associated diagnostic criteria is essential for accurate identification and appropriate management of patients whose anxiety symptoms are linked to underlying medical or physiological conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1634
retrieved_docs.content: Anxiety disorder due to another medical condition is classified as a specific type of anxiety disorders and is defined by particular diagnostic criteria that guide its identification and treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2766
retrieved_docs.content: Psychological disorder (اضطراب نفسي) is a broad category of mental health conditions that encompasses more specific disorders such as depression (اكتئاب) and separation‑anxiety disorder (اضطراب قلق الانفصال), each of which is a narrower subtype within the overall class of psychological disorders. Both the general category of psychological disorder and its child conditions—depression and separation‑anxiety disorder—are identified as targets for prevention and control measures, indicating that strategies aimed at preventing onset, reducing severity, and managing symptoms are applicable across the spectrum of these mental health conditions. This hierarchical relationship underscores the importance of applying preventive and control interventions at both the overarching disorder level and the individual disorder level to improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2292
retrieved_docs.content: Anxiety disorder due to another medical condition, also described as anxiety disorder due to a known physiological condition, is a distinct psychiatric diagnosis that requires the presence of specific diagnostic criteria. This disorder is characterized by anxiety symptoms that are directly attributable to an underlying medical or physiological condition, and clinicians must identify the causal medical condition and apply the established criteria to confirm the diagnosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2288
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders usually diagnosed in infancy are understood to have a broader relationship with ذُهان الطفولة غير المحدد، حالة راهنة أو نشطة, which denotes an unspecified childhood psychosis that is presently active.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 4 -- Community 2898
retrieved_docs.content: Mental disorder (اضطراب نفسي) is a broad diagnostic category that encompasses more specific conditions such as depression (اكتئاب) and separation anxiety disorder (اضطراب قلق الانفصال). Separation anxiety disorder is identified as a narrower form within the overall class of mental disorders, while depression is listed as a child concept of mental disorder, indicating its status as a distinct subtype. Epidemiology is recognized as an appropriate qualifier for mental disorder, depression, and separation anxiety disorder, underscoring the relevance of epidemiological research in describing the prevalence, distribution, and risk factors of these psychiatric conditions. This hierarchical organization and the emphasis on epidemiological data together support a comprehensive understanding of how these disorders are related and inform patient care and public‑health strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2293
retrieved_docs.content: Other specified transient mental disorders due to conditions classified elsewhere is categorized as a subcategory within the broader class of anxiety disorders, indicating that it falls under the parent classification of anxiety disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 428
retrieved_docs.content: Anxiety disorder due to another medical condition is defined using the diagnostic criteria established for anxiety disorder due to a known physiological condition, and these criteria are incorporated into the broader diagnostic framework for anxiety disorders. Within the classification system, the Arabic term “الذهان” (psychosis) includes a child category called “حالات ذُهانية عضوية عابرة” (transient organic psychotic conditions). Both anxiety disorders and mood disorders are described as classifying or encompassing these transient organic psychotic conditions, indicating that such conditions can be viewed under either anxiety‑related or mood‑related diagnostic groups. The category “Other specified transient mental disorders due to conditions classified elsewhere” is linked to both mood disorders and anxiety disorders as parent categories, reflecting its dual affiliation. Additionally, the specific entity “Transient organic psychotic condition, depressive type” is reported to be closely related to mood disorders and may even be synonymous with them. Together, these relationships illustrate the overlapping nature of certain transient psychotic and depressive presentations within both anxiety and mood disorder classifications, emphasizing the importance of recognizing shared diagnostic criteria and hierarchical links for accurate clinical assessment and treatment planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 1 -- Community 39
retrieved_docs.content: AODR mental disorder as a child concept, indicating a hierarchical relationship within substance‑related conditions. In the broader nosology, “other psychiatric disorders” encompass both اضطراب الكرب التالي للرض and رُهاب (phobia). Developmental disorders are positioned as a subset within the larger class of other psychiatric disorders, highlighting their classification as a specific type of psychiatric condition. Collectively, these relationships emphasize the importance of careful medication selection and monitoring in psychiatric care, particularly when prescribing MAOIs, opioids, or agents with overlapping toxicity profiles, and they illustrate how various psychiatric diagnoses are organized within hierarchical diagnostic frameworks.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 1357
retrieved_docs.content: Anxiety disorder due to another medical condition is classified within anxiety disorders and adheres to the diagnostic criteria established for anxiety disorders. Other specified transient mental disorders due to conditions classified elsewhere is also situated under the anxiety disorders category, identified as a subcategory (has parent) of anxiety disorders. Both entities illustrate how specific mental health conditions are organized under the broader anxiety disorders classification, emphasizing the role of diagnostic criteria and hierarchical relationships in accurate diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 2677
retrieved_docs.content: Anxiety disorders serve as a classification for حالات ذُهانية عضوية عابرة, which are transient organic psychotic conditions. This relationship indicates that brief, organic‑based psychotic episodes are considered within the broader category of anxiety disorders, highlighting an overlap between anxiety symptomatology and short‑lasting psychotic states. Recognizing that حالات ذُهانية عضوية عابرة fall under anxiety disorders can help clinicians appropriately assess and manage patients who present with transient psychotic features in the context of anxiety‑related pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 2 -- Community 1009
retrieved_docs.content: The Arabic category الهَذَيان includes as a child the condition حالات ذُهانية عضوية عابرة. Both anxiety disorders and mood disorders are used to classify حالات ذُهانية عضوية عابرة, indicating that this transient organic psychotic condition can be viewed within the spectra of both anxiety and mood pathology. The diagnostic category Other specified transient mental disorders due to conditions classified elsewhere is linked to both mood disorders and anxiety disorders as a parent classification, reflecting its placement under these broader disorder groups. Additionally, the specific entity Transient organic psychotic condition, depressive type is described as related to and possibly synonymous with mood disorders, further emphasizing the overlap between depressive‑type transient psychotic presentations and mood disorder classifications. Together, these relationships illustrate the interconnected nature of transient organic psychotic conditions, anxiety disorders, and mood disorders within psychiatric taxonomy, highlighting the importance of recognizing shared features for accurate diagnosis and treatment planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2452
retrieved_docs.content: Gabapentin, an anticonvulsant and neuropathic pain medication, may be employed in the therapeutic management of رُهاب (phobia). Although primarily indicated for seizures and neuropathic pain, gabapentin is sometimes used off‑label to alleviate anxiety‑related symptoms associated with specific phobic disorders, providing clinicians with an additional pharmacologic option for patients who experience heightened fear responses. This potential application underscores the broader utility of gabapentin in addressing certain anxiety conditions beyond its conventional uses.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 2361
retrieved_docs.content: Headache; including migraine is classified as a condition that belongs to the broader category of Nervous system and sense organ diseases. The category Nervous system and sense organ diseases also includes the child entity اضطراب العين (eye disorder). Recognizing that both headache—including migraine—and eye disorders are subtypes of Nervous system and sense organ diseases helps clinicians organize related symptoms, consider shared pathophysiological mechanisms, and guide appropriate diagnostic and therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 2 -- Community 677
retrieved_docs.content: Transient organic psychotic conditions (حالات ذُهانية عضوية عابرة) are recognized as a subtype of organic psychosis (ذُهان عضوي) and are classified within both anxiety disorders and mood disorders. The diagnostic category “Other specified transient mental disorders due to conditions classified elsewhere” is linked as a child of both mood disorders and anxiety disorders, indicating that it shares features of each. The depressive type of transient organic psychotic condition (Transient organic psychotic condition, depressive type) is closely related to mood disorders and may be considered synonymous with mood‑disorder presentations. Together, these relationships illustrate that certain transient mental disorders span the domains of anxiety, mood, and organic psychosis, highlighting the importance of an integrated diagnostic approach for accurate classification and optimal patient care.
